story:
An octopus rises through its tank like an arpeggio up staff paper, and Bob—composer, middle-aged, alert to patterns—hears Jesu, Joy of Man’s Desiring in its climb, a melody with discordant notes that somehow speak rather than jar. He orchestrates the scene in his head: flashing fish as pizzicato violins, bottom-bumping groupers for bass. He thinks Middle Age, Middle C, Middle Sea, and leaves the aquarium hungry for a tuna sandwich and full of the idea that all movement might be music.

On the white-striped crosswalk outside, people step like notes and in-between notes: a skateboarder glissando, the old woman from the exhibit staccato. He imagines a Glenn Gould video—superimposed keyboard, pedestrians “playing” it—and, after a long drought, feels excited again. He steps onto a thick white stripe, hears the C below C—and a bus hits him. Dragged thirty yards, he becomes that terrible, inevitable chord: Man meets Fate. Beethoven. Verdi’s La forza del destino. Do you add a cymbal crash or let the silence after say it all?

Security cameras clear the driver; Bob had walked into the path. Colleagues, punning through grief, call it an “accidental,” Terry O’Neill muttering, “to a double flat.” Tess, who had stood nearby, sees it happen and faints, her cheek striking the yellow bollard—a grace note, Bob would have said. When she can stand, she cannot look away from the arm that has come to rest under the bus, palm up as if cradling, far down the street, a tiny church spire. She has a thriving graphic design business, too thriving, really; she needs staff and keeps postponing it. She breathes slowly and wonders if she will go into shock.

Tess would have suited Bob. That’s not “Interestingly” but something harder to name. She dies nine years later of metastatic breast cancer. At her funeral, where the casket can be open—unlike Bob’s—her sister speaks beautifully, but an old boyfriend rises uninvited and says what everyone recognizes as truer still. Listeners leave the church thinking of Tess as an unfinished portrait—introversion and quirkiness were only first strokes—and some twenty people, walking into their own errands, wonder who really sees them.

Now imagine Bob surviving that crosswalk. He would have smiled at the pretty, shy woman by the freshly painted bollard and said, “Good morning,” then, gently: the aquarium is worth it, there’s an octopus who moves beautifully, it made him think. Tess—black hornrims, French braid, tweed skirt like aerial farmland, battered black Converse, no makeup because none needed—might have answered, “Oh? Like what?” And that small door would have swung wide: synesthesia; movement-as-music; safety and curiosity growing into coffee, then dates, then first kiss, first embrace. Not Mysterious Barricades—Mysterious Open Channels. She would choose sky-blue, not white, when she marries him in a field, honoring the old love who spoke so well at her funeral in that other version of the story; he would understand, which is why she would say yes.

Bob would teach theory at the university, write concertos on commission; a classical DJ would brighten rush hour with “Robert Richardson’s Piano Concerto No. 3,” Yuja Wang at the piano, Dudamel conducting. Parties would be Bob’s domain; the kitchen, Tess’s with Rose and Maeve. And the wedding music? It would be the music the octopus wrote. Whether good or bad by our measure doesn’t matter. Among octopi, it might be as beautiful as anything in the universe.
